name: Gaudapada
dates_notes: Traditionally Śaṅkara's parama-guru (teacher's teacher); on the modern Hacker / Nakamura dating of Śaṅkara to 700–750, Gauḍapāda is now placed late-6th to mid-7th c. (Bhattacharya, King). Academic dating from internal Buddhist-philosophical references (engagement with developed Yogācāra and Madhyamaka).
core_thesis: Gauḍapāda's central thesis is *ajāti-vāda*: the doctrine of non-origination ([Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā 3.48, 4.71](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.48, 4.71)). He argues it within a verse-exegesis of the Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad's four-fold *ātman* — *vaiśvānara* (waking), *taijasa* (dream), *prājña* (deep sleep), *turīya* (the fourth) (Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad 3–7; Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā 1.1–10). *Turīya* corresponds to the silent *amātra* fourth of *Oṃ* and is the non-dual ground (Māṇḍūkya-Upaniṣad 12; Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā 1.16–18). The first major argumentative move is the *vaitathya* (unreality) argument of *prakaraṇa* 2: the *jāgrat* and *svapna* states are epistemically symmetrical because both are mind-projected, and what is not present at the beginning and end is also not present in the middle ([Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā 2.5–6](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.5-6)). *Turīya* alone is *advaita*, free of cause and effect, *ajaḥ* (unborn) ([Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā 3.31–37](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.31-37)). The second major move is causal: in [*Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā* 3.19–21](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.19-21) he argues that if the unborn deathless were really differentiated, it would have to become mortal — which is impossible. Hence [*Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā* 4](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/4).71's conclusion that no *jīva* is ever truly born. The third move is the *alāta-cakra* analogy: apparent multiplicity is the trace of an unmoving consciousness, as a firebrand whirled in a circle traces a fiery ring that has no real existence ([Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā 4.47–52](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/4.47-52)). Mahāyāna analytic vocabulary (*catuṣkoṭi*, the dialectic of origination) appears throughout ([Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā 4.83–84](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/4.83-84)), but the unborn ground here is *ātman*-*brahman*, positively asserted ([Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā 3.32–33](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.32-33)), not the Mādhyamika *śūnya*. Liberation is *pratibodha* — recognition of one's identity with the unborn fourth — not a process of becoming ([Māṇḍūkya-Kārikā 1.16; 4.96–98](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.16; 4.96-98)).

engaged_works.title: Mandukya-Karika
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored as a unified work, though authorship of the four prakaraṇas individually has been disputed; modern consensus accepts Gauḍapādan authorship of all four.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus on Gauḍapādan authorship of the unified four-prakaraṇa text.

engaged_works.summary: A verse *prakaraṇa* in four parts; also titled *Āgama-Śāstra*. *Āgama-Prakaraṇa* (1) is the most directly Upaniṣadic, glossing the twelve verses of the Māṇḍūkya on the four states of *ātman* and the four quarters of *Oṃ*. *Vaitathya-Prakaraṇa* (2) sets out the unreality argument: waking and dream are mind-projected and epistemically equivalent; the well-known reasoning at [MK 2.5–6](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/2.5-6) turns on the parity of differentiations and on the inference that what is absent at the beginning and end of an experience is no different in the middle. *Advaita-Prakaraṇa* (3) gives the positive exposition of non-duality, the doctrine of *ajāti*, and the simile of space delimited by pots (*ghaṭākāśa*) at [MK 3.3–7](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.3-7) — the classic anchor for Gauḍapāda's account of the *jīva*-*ātman* relation. *Alātaśānti-Prakaraṇa* (4) develops the firebrand-circle argument and engages Buddhist analytic categories most heavily. Authorship is contested. Traditionally the four are by a single Gauḍapāda, Śaṅkara's *parama-guru*. Modern scholarship has variously argued for separate authors, especially for a Buddhist hand on the fourth *prakaraṇa* (Bhattacharya, V. Bhattacharya 1943; King 1995). Current consensus broadly accepts unified Gauḍapādan authorship while recognizing the fourth *prakaraṇa*'s heaviest Mahāyāna intertextuality. The work is the foundational pre-Śaṅkara Advaita treatise; Śaṅkara's commentary on it canonizes its place in the Advaita *prasthāna*.

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
anādimāyayā supto yadā jīvaḥ prabudhyate
ajam anidram asvapnam advaitaṃ budhyate tadā //

prapañco yadi vidyeta nivarteta na saṃśayaḥ
māyāmātram idaṃ dvaitam advaitaṃ paramārthataḥ //

vikalpo vinivarteta kalpito yadi kenacit
upadeśād ayaṃ vādo jñāte dvaitaṃ na vidyate //

pada_analysis.morphology: abl sg masc (surface sandhi of upadeśāt before ayaṃ)

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: anādi ca sā māyā ca; tayā
paramaḥ arthaḥ yasmin saḥ paramārthaḥ; tataḥ
samasa_vigrahas.note: adverbial ablative: from the standpoint of ultimate reality

karaka_structure.note: agent of awakening
instrumental/circumstantial condition of sleep
object known upon awakening
subject of hypothetical existence and cessation
subject of cessation in the conditional claim
instruction as the basis for this teaching
subject of the existential negation after knowledge

key_passages.english_close: When the jīva, asleep through beginningless māyā, awakens, then it comes to know the unborn, sleepless, dreamless non-dual. If the manifold world were real, it would cease; there is no doubt. This duality is mere māyā; non-duality is the ultimate reality. Conceptual construction would cease, if it had been projected by anyone; this teaching is by way of instruction. When That is known, duality is not found.
key_passages.why_this_passage: These verses are representative because they tie together Gauḍapāda's account of bondage, awakening, the status of duality, and the pedagogical function of teaching in one compact sequence. This is not a detached slogan: it shows how non-duality is recognized, how the world is classified, and why Vedāntic instruction still operates within that framework.
key_passages.source_edition: Vinayaka Ganesha Apate, ed., Āgamaśāstra / Gauḍapādakārikā (Pune: Anandasrama Sanskrit Series 10, 1921), as transcribed in /orcd/home/002/eeshan/philosophy/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/gaudapada_mandukya_karika_gretil.txt (GRETIL version dated 2020-07-31).

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
svapnajāgaritasthāne hy ekam āhur manīṣiṇaḥ
bhedānāṃ hi samatvena prasiddhenaiva hetunā //

ādāv ante ca yan nāsti vartamāne 'pi tat tathā
vitathaiḥ sadṛśāḥ santo avitathā iva lakṣitāḥ //

pada_analysis.gloss: in the domain of dream and waking

pada_analysis.morphology: particle (sandhi form of hi before vowel)
indecl negation + 3sg pres ind P sandhi
loc sg neut pres ptc Ā

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: svapnaṃ ca jāgaritaṃ ca tayoḥ sthānam

karaka_structure.note: domain under discussion
what the wise say dream and waking to be
agents of the statement
instrumental reason: parity of the differentiations
subject of the existential proposition
temporal locations structuring the argument

key_passages.english_close: The wise have said that dream-state and waking-state are one. They say so on the well-known reason: the parity of the differentiations [in the two]. What is not present at the beginning and is not present at the end is likewise not present in the middle either. Being similar to unreal things, they are nevertheless perceived as if they were not unreal.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: unreality or falsity disclosed by analysis)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This is a fair selection because it gives Gauḍapāda's reasoning for the dream-waking parity claim rather than only the later conclusion that everything is unreal. The verse pair makes visible the argumentative move that destabilizes ordinary realism and grounds the rest of the text's non-dual analysis.
key_passages.source_edition: Vinayaka Ganesha Apate, ed., Āgamaśāstra / Gauḍapādakārikā (Pune: Anandasrama Sanskrit Series 10, 1921), as transcribed in /orcd/home/002/eeshan/philosophy/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/gaudapada_mandukya_karika_gretil.txt (GRETIL version dated 2020-07-31).

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
ātmā hy ākāśavaj jīvair ghaṭākāśair ivoditaḥ
ghaṭādivac ca saṃghātair jātāv etan nidarśanam //

ghaṭādiṣu pralīneṣu ghaṭākāśādayo yathā
ākāśe saṃpralīyante tadvaj jīvā ihātmani //

yathaikasmin ghaṭākāśe rajodhūmādibhir yute
na sarve saṃprayujyante tadvaj jīvāḥ sukhādibhiḥ //

rūpakāryasamākhyāś ca bhidyante tatra tatra vai
ākāśasya na bhedo 'sti tadvaj jīveṣu nirṇayaḥ //

nākāśasya ghaṭākāśo vikārāvayavau yathā
naivātmanaḥ sadā jīvo vikārāvayavau tathā //

pada_analysis.morphology: particle (sandhi form of hi before vowel)
particle + nom sg masc ppp sandhi

pada_analysis.gloss: as in pots and the like

pada_analysis.morphology: loc sg fem (surface sandhi of jātau before etan)

pada_analysis.gloss: with dust, smoke, and the like

samasa_vigrahas.note: space as delimited by a pot

samasa_vigrahas.resolution: rajaḥ ca dhūmaḥ ca ādi ca taiḥ
rūpaṃ ca kāryaṃ ca samākhyā ca

samasa_vigrahas.note: the space seemingly delimited by a pot
paired predicates denied of pot-space and jīva

karaka_structure.note: subject of the analogy as presented
instrumental comparison through apparent multiplicity
aggregates as the comparative instrument for origination language
subjects that merge in the analogy
locus into which they merge
all pot-spaces are the subject of non-association
adjuncts that do not transfer to all
plural factors that differ from case to case
genitives governing the denial of difference, modification, and parthood

key_passages.english_close: The Self, indeed, is here presented as like space, with the jīvas presented as like pot-spaces. And, with respect to origination from aggregates as from pots and the like, this is the illustration [for the jīvas' apparent multiplicity]. Just as, when pots and the like dissolve. Pot-spaces and the rest re-merge into space. So too do the jīvas here re-merge into the Self. Just as, when one particular pot-space is conjoined with dust, smoke, and the like. Not all [pot-spaces] are thereby conjoined. So likewise, jīvas are not all conjoined with one [jīva's] pleasures and pains. Forms, functions, and corresponding names differ from place to place. But of space itself there is no real difference; the conclusion with respect to jīvas is the same. As pot-space is not a modification or a part of space, so likewise the jīva is never a modification or a part of the Self.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: pot-space, the classic image of apparently delimited space)

key_passages.why_this_passage: This passage is representative because Gauḍapāda does not merely assert identity; he works out, through the pot-space analogy, how plurality, dissolution, adjunct-specific experience, and non-parthood are all to be understood together. The sequence is a core Advaita-prakaraṇa exposition of the jīva/ātman relation, not a detachable ornament.
key_passages.source_edition: Vinayaka Ganesha Apate, ed., Āgamaśāstra / Gauḍapādakārikā (Pune: Anandasrama Sanskrit Series 10, 1921), as transcribed in /orcd/home/002/eeshan/philosophy/materials/primary_texts/sanskrit/vedanta/full_corpus/gaudapada_mandukya_karika_gretil.txt (GRETIL version dated 2020-07-31).

key_passages.sanskrit_iast:
māyayā bhidyate hy etan nānyathājaṃ kathaṃcana
tattvato bhidyamāne hi martyatām amṛtaṃ vrajet //

ajātasyaiva bhāvasya jātim icchanti vādinaḥ
ajāto hy amṛto bhāvo martyatāṃ katham eṣyati //

na bhavaty amṛtaṃ martyaṃ na martyam amṛtaṃ tathā
prakṛter anyathābhāvo na kathaṃcid bhaviṣyati //

pada_analysis.morphology: particle (sandhi form of hi before vowel)
indecl negation + indecl adverb + acc sg neut sandhi
loc sg neut pres ptc Ā
particle (sandhi form of hi before vowel)

karaka_structure.note: instrument of apparent differentiation
object said to be differentiated only by māyā
subject that would enter mortality if real differentiation occurred
birth posited by the disputants
agents who posit origination
subject of the rhetorical future eṣyati
genitive constraining the denied alteration of nature

key_passages.english_close: This unborn reality is differentiated by māyā, indeed, not otherwise in any way; for, if it were being differentiated in reality, the deathless would pass into mortality. The disputants posit birth for a reality that is itself unborn; yet how would an unborn, deathless reality go to mortality? The deathless does not become mortal, nor likewise the mortal deathless; becoming otherwise than one's intrinsic nature will not occur in any way.

preserved_terms.first_use_gloss: (roughly: appearance-producing differentiation that does not alter reality itself)
(roughly: the reality or existent under discussion)

key_passages.why_this_passage: These verses are a fair basis for Gauḍapāda's causal metaphysics because they argue for ajāti by showing what real origination would entail: the deathless would have to become mortal, which is impossible. The passage therefore captures the logic of non-origination rather than only citing the famous conclusion that nothing is born.
